Cory Strand.title: Anxiety
Cory Strand.about: A collection of covers recorded over the last three years. I've called the compilation "Anxiety" because much of this material was recorded as an attempt to deal with my personal struggle with anxiety and depression. Working on creative things keeps my mind from wandering into dark recesses of self-loathing.
Cory Strand.credits:
released March 20, 2015
All instruments and vocals by Cory Strand. Recorded on shitty equipment at home.

Cory Strand.title: Assault on Precinct 13: A Reinterpretation
Cory Strand.about: One of my favorite drone reworks! Hard to believe I hadn't put this one up yet. Originally released as AOW 161, long since sold out. Completed in a single marathon session in 2014. Vastly different working methods from where I'm at today today. I wanted it to have a fast and dirty/visceral sort of feel (as much as drone can be visceral). A rework of Carpenter's classic minimalist synth score. A white hot night of DRONE!
Cory Strand.credits:
released March 6, 2021
Music by CS, January 22nd, 2014.

Cory Strand.title: Auto Focus: Complete
Cory Strand.about:
Another set of extremely long-from recordings crafted out of elements from and related to Paul Schrader's superb film "Auto Focus," about the life and bizarre death of actor Bob Crane. One of my favorites and a deeply underrated film. Definitely one to check out if you've not seen it.
This was originally released as a fairly lavish box set through Altar Of Waste Records in 2014; each piece was again issued on DVD-R data discs due to the length. I've necessarily split each suite up into smaller sections for digital release.
I've given each track here it's own image as well, pulled from the film.
Total runtime: 26 hrs, 45 min.
Here's my original write-up of the box set, from 2014:
"Massive box set compiling an enormous amount of manipulation work sourced from Paul Schrader's 2002 film "Auto Focus." Based on Robert Graysmith's book, "Auto Focus" tells the story of all-around nice guy actor Bob Crane (of "Hogan's Heroes" fame) and his extremely rampant sex drive. Schrader's film is phenomenal, a beautifully shot descent into pure and unadulterated enjoyment, a portrait of one man's struggle to reconcile his unhinged private life with his innocuous public persona.
"Auto Focus: Complete" features six discs of very long-form HNW and ambient pieces crafted from the audio track of the film as well from various audio ephemera associated with the film and Bob Crane. The ambient works here are easily some of the most beautifully empty and ghostly pieces i've made, taken to exhaustive lengths to fully immerse you in the ether. The HNW works follow the pattern of "(Totality)" recordings that I've been doing lately with other films, taking giant swathes of audio and manipulating them into super textured, constantly mutating psychedelic wipeouts. "Auto Focus: Complete" is nearly 30 hours in its total run time-there is an immense amount of material to lose yourself in! I am incredibly proud of this one-it took an enormous amount of time and effort to create, and I think the results are well worth it. Anyone into my recent manipulations will not be disappointed!"
"It's Fuck Time," The Murder of Bob Crane," and "Auto Focus (Totality)" are manipulations of the film's entire audio track.
"I Thought You Liked It" is sourced from Angelo Badalamenti's score for the film.
"Don't Make Waves" is sourced from Bobby Goldboro's song "These Are the Best Times" off of the "Superdad" soundtrack, one of Bob Crane's post-"Hogan's Heroes" film roles, which has a deep metaphorical aspect in Schrader's film.
"Jouissance" is sourced from the "Hogan's Heroes" theme.
This is one of my best, most intense works. It is truly sound as total exhaustive wipeout. March towards death.
-Cory Strand, January 2020
Cory Strand.credits:
released January 9, 2020
Everything by CS.

Cory Strand.title: Blue Velvet: The Complete Series, 2013 - 2016
Cory Strand.about:
One of the projects I always hoped that Altar Of Waste would be associated with.
Working with the idea of David Lynch was one of my initial, and strongest, inspirations as an HNW and drone composer; my work has always been as deeply influenced by him as much as by Eliane Radigue. And he has long been one of my very favorite filmmakers.
The "Blue Velvet" series was an open-door compilation, designed as a platform for both established HNW acts and newcomers to the scene.
As long as an artist was a true fan of the film and could do quality work specific to and inspired by it, they could contribute an album to the series.
The initial volumes were specifically curated; each artist was selected by me and given a track from Angelo Badalamenti's score to work with.
The results were inspiring; every artist gave it their all.
I quickly realized that this project could be an ongoing one, given the film's, and the label's, reach.
The final volume of the series was turned in in 2016.
I am very proud of this series; it truly captured what Altar Of Waste was about.
This series, and my label, was about a love for cinema, a love for extreme music of all genres, and an unbridled creativity unfettered by genre constraints or expectations.
I despise the notion of what HNW "should" be and I wholly reject any attempt to define it. Nothingness transcends boundary, and gatekeeping is the lowest form of criticism. It is pathetic and insecure. Altar Of Waste was always meant as a "fuck you" to expectation, convention, and definition.
I still live by that.
I hate scenes, i hate cliques, and i hate those who work to establish and perpetuate them. I hate those who take no stand in favor of some sort of pointless "collectiveness." I hate the idea of the "HNW fam." It is completely illusory. It's all bullshit, and it sickens me.
I hate those who have done nothing, given nothing, and expect everything. That's all I ever encountered anymore in the last two years of AOW's existence.
I hate ego and I hate the sense of self-congratulatory circle-jerking that typifies today's HNW world.
This series was a joy to work on - digging through the interwebs and sourcing all sorts of images and posters for the film from around the world. No one image or design could really define the film...for this digital release i've chosen one that seems to convey a sort of sense of "expanse," but by no means or measure is it a summary or an encapsulation. The film is so much larger than that; I offer only an approximation, based on my own reading and enjoyment (for me, a sense of mystery, "unknowableness," and sadness.) Which is what the series ultimately was for all the artists involved (I hope.)
It is my privilege to offer this series in its entirety now.
Take it for free - it is my gift to you.
For this digital release, I have added a Eugene Critchley track (completed in 2020.)
Also, just for the record, I contributed a few pieces to this compilation under various projects: the Cory Strand, Lethe, and Dejection tracks are all my work.
Curated by me, 2013 - 2016.
-Cory Strand, January 2020
Cory Strand.credits: released January 6, 2020

Cory Strand.title: Boogie Nights: Complete
Cory Strand.about:
One of my final large-scale box sets released under the AOW banner. No one was interested in this set when it was released, and I never sold a single copy (understandable, given its expense); I made a copy for a friend and that's the only physical that exists. These recordings were made during a shitty time in my life and I was throwing myself into work to try and get through it. Originally released in 2016 as a 27CD + DVD-R box set.
It's fair to say that "Boogie Nights" is one of my favorite films of all time, and one of the films that inspired me to go to film school and study the medium academically.
Cory Strand.credits:
released March 7, 2021
Music by CS.

Cory Strand.title: Carpenter Abandoned: The Base Tracks
Cory Strand.about:
These pieces were originally completed in 2014 as base ambient tracks for a collaborative album with Wet Dream Asphyxiation. That collab never happened, sadly, but I released these tracks a few years later as the "bonus album" included with my subscription series, "A Year Of Carpenter."
These compositions are pulled from all of JC's scoring work; some of them I had done previous, on my own Carpenter reworks, some were drawn from films I hadn't yet worked with. The tracks that I had worked with previously were done again from the start, with new manipulations and techniques to differentiate them from my original versions.
When i grabbed these files a few nights ago i noticed that two tracks were lost, "Assault On Precinct 13" and "Main Title/Engulfed Cathedral." In an effort to provide as complete a restructuring of the original album as possible, I have gone back and remade new versions of these tracks, adhering as closely as i could (remember) to my working methods/techniques from several years ago. I think i came pretty close.
1. From "Assault On Precinct 13"
2. From "The Fog"
3. From "Escape From New York"
4. From "The Fog"
5. From "Halloween" and "Halloween II"
6. From "They Live"
7. From "Christine"
8. From "Dark Star"
9. From "Prince Of Darkness"
10. From "Halloween III"
11. From "They Live"
12. From "Escape From L.A."
13. From "They Live"
14. From "Vampires"
Cory Strand.credits:
released July 2, 2019
Drones by CS.

Cory Strand.title: Fargo: The Long-Form Compositions
Cory Strand.about: A collection of my three long-form "Fargo" reworks from 2014. Originally released through Altar Of Waste Records in scant editions, though none sold through their runs. Cory Strand.title: From Chaos, December 2015
Cory Strand.about:
In December of 2015, Thomas Havales invited me to program a playlist of my work for his "Guests From Chaos" podcast. The selected artist would curate a two hour playlist of their work, plus a few artists they considered influences. I took this as a really unique opportunity to comb through what was already a pretty exhaustive personal discography and highlight some of what I felt was my strongest work up to that point. I wanted to make my playlist something special, so I mixed the pieces down into a seamless chunk of music, moving from HNW (which is what i was know for then through Altar Of Waste) through my ambient and drone work. I still feel the pieces selected represent me well, and I love the mix. It wasn't that long ago, but my work doesn't really sound like this anymore, and sometimes I miss that. I was finding my voice back then. I was much less self-critical. Sometimes that was bad and indulgent (an adjective that could be fairly levied against me even today), but sometimes it was good. Some of the ambient stuff here is some of the best I've ever made. I always wanted it to be pretty, and sad, and somehow maybe transcendent. I only included one "influential" work in the mix - Wendy Carlos and Rachel Elkind's theme for "The Shining." Those who know my work will know why.
I never heard any feedback about the show afterwards. I don't know if anyone listened, or cared. But i still like it very much.
Due to length I had to split the podcast into two pieces, but I've done my best to retain its "flow."
Here's the complete tracklist (everything segues):
- Cory Strand – Main Title (Terminator 2: A Reinterpretation CDR/Occult Supremacy Productions/2014)
- Cory Strand – Cataclysm (excerpt) (High Off My Love: The Collection 8CDR/Altar Of Waste Records/2015)
- Cory Strand – Dream Attack (A Nightmare On Elm Street: A Reinterpretation 2CDR/Occult Supremacy Productions/2013)
- Cory Strand – Halleluwah (excerpt) (The Bling Ring: A Reinterpretation 6CDR/Altar Of Waste Records/2014)
- Cory Strand – Starlight Glimmers, Dreams Wither Away (excerpt) (Three Pieces For Lindsay CDR/Occult Supremacy Productions/2013)
- Cory Strand – This Life Is Not What I Imagined It To Be (excerpt) (Three Pieces For Lindsay CDR/Occult Supremacy Productions/2013)
- Cory Strand – Fargo, North Dakota (excerpt) (Fargo: A Reinterpretation 5CDR/Altar Of Waste Records/2014)
- Cory Strand – Bemidji, MN (excerpt) (Fargo: Revisited 10CDR/Altar Of Waste Records/2014)
- Cory Strand – The Bison (excerpt) (To The Wonder: A Reinterpretation 5CDR/Altar Of Waste Records/2013)
- Cory Strand – Polish Poem (excerpt) (Inland Empire: A Reinterpretation 4CDR/Altar Of Waste Records Private Release/2013)
- Cory Strand – Good Time (Lovely and Inane Remix) (excerpt) (Paris, Do You Speak French? 4CDR/Altar Of Waste Records/2014)
- Cory Strand – Frostbite/Cold and Dead (excerpt) (Nar/Cory Strand Split 4CDR/Altar Of Waste Records/2014)
- Cory Strand – Escape From New York Main Title/Engulfed Cathedral (excerpt) (Unreleased/2013)
- Wendy Carlos and Rachel Elkind – The Shining Main Title (The Shining OST LP/Warner Brothers Records/1980)
- Cory Strand – Main Title (excerpt) (The Shining: A Reinterpretation CDR/Altar Of Waste Records/2012)
- Cory Strand – Hermione (excerpt) (Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince: A Reinterpretation 20CDR/Altar Of Waste Records/2013)

Cory Strand.title: Fuck Off Nowadays HNW
Cory Strand.about:
This is my contribution for a private tape swap i'm involved in for 2019.
This album is directed at certain people. Your trauma is not a substitute for meaningful art. Your pain is not greater than anyone else's, nor does it make the "art" you produce any less vacant. Everything you've done has been done before, and better. Your work will be forgotten, as will every "live performance" you've ever done. All that will be left is the black hole of hurt that you constantly demand everyone around you acknowledge.
A spotlight is not an illumination. A platform is not transcendence.
I hate your scene, and I hate your projects.
I am not a part of it.
I refuse, and I deny.
Death to false noise.
FUCK OFF AND DIE.
Cory Strand.credits:
released April 26, 2019
Composed, performed, and recorded by Eugene Critchley.
Cover art by Zachary Ledsinger.

Cory Strand.title: Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince: A Reinterpretation, Vol. I
Cory Strand.about:
If "The Girl With The Dragon Tattoo: A Reinterpretation" was my second masterpiece, this was my first, and the one closest to my heart. Originally created in 2013, this album captured all the ambition and drive I had in the early days of Altar Of Waste and best showcased my vision of what an HNW label could produce when freed from pitiful genre expectations.
And they *are* truly pitiful.
This was a massive "fuck you" to expectation, and I can't properly convey the amount of shit I took from people when I released this.
My work doesn't sound like this anymore; my methodology was different, my approach was different. I was younger and perhaps more experimental then. I was new to the scene. I just wanted to make beautiful and interesting music in honor of the cinema and scores I loved, and I wanted my label to reflect that love and openness, my willingness to go further than anyone else.
This is quite simply one of the best things I've ever made.
"Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince" is not just my favorite film in the HP series, but one of my favorite films of all time. It's perfect.
Originally released in 2013 as a 26 disc box set.
Sounds culled from the score, the film, and my own synthesizer explorations.
Cory Strand.credits:
released December 28, 2019
Manipulation, construction, and synthesizers by CS.

"Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince" is not just my favorite film in the HP series, but one of my favorite films of all time. It's perfect.
Originally released in 2013 as a 26 disc box set.
Sounds culled from the score, the film, and my own synthesizer explorations.
Cory Strand.credits:
released December 27, 2019
Manipulation, construction, and synthesizers by CS.

Cory Strand.title: Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince: A Reinterpretation, Vol. V
Cory Strand.about:
If "The Girl With The Dragon Tattoo: A Reinterpretation" was my second masterpiece, this was my first, and the one closest to my heart. Originally created in 2013, this album captured all the ambition and drive I had in the early days of Altar Of Waste and best showcased my vision of what an HNW label could produce when freed from pitiful genre expectations.
And they *are* truly pitiful.
This was a massive "fuck you" to expectation, and I can't properly convey the amount of shit I took from people when I released this.
My work doesn't sound like this anymore; my methodology was different, my approach was different. I was younger and perhaps more experimental then. I was new to the scene. I just wanted to make beautiful and interesting music in honor of the cinema and scores I loved, and I wanted my label to reflect that love and openness, my willingness to go further than anyone else.
This is quite simply one of the best things I've ever made.
"Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince" is not just my favorite film in the HP series, but one of my favorite films of all time. It's perfect.
Originally released in 2013 as a 26 disc box set.
Sounds culled from the score, the film, and my own synthesizer explorations.
Cory Strand.credits:
released December 27, 2019
Manipulation, construction, and synthesizers by CS.

Cory Strand.title: I Saw You Like A Summer Dream
Cory Strand.about:
A sequel of sorts to my contribution to the Infinity Series from a few years back. Whereas that album took elements of Meat Loaf's "Bat Out Of Hell II" album and transformed them into progressive HNW, this album is a full-on, track by track drone reworking of it.
Much thanks to my friend JK for building me up back then. It's never been forgotten.
Cory Strand.credits:
released December 19, 2020
Music by CS.

This track was intended as my side of a split release with Fleuve Noir. I don't know that said split will happen now.

Sad and hopeless ambient.
Possible self-released physical version coming soon.

Cory Strand.title: Leave This World For Awhile
Cory Strand.about: I've loved Tom Petty since high school. I saw him live with my Dad several years back and it was one of the best shows I've ever seen. I was very sad to hear of his passing. This album is a handful of my favorite TP tracks ("Free Fallin'," "It's Good To Be King," and "Don't Come Around Here No More") reworked into aching drones, my way of saying thanks to the man for giving me so many great experiences and memories.
Cory Strand.credits:
released October 21, 2017
Manipulation and construction by CS.

Cory Strand.title: "Les Miserables"
Cory Strand.about:
Source recordings for a collaborative release with The Ebony Tower based on Raymond Bernard's film/Victor Hugo's novel "Les Miserables," begun in February of 2017.
The collaboration is still in the works and will see a limited physical release in deluxe packaging.
I have always felt these tracks are interesting, eerie, and beautiful in and of themselves.
Released with permission, in this form, from MR.

released September 13, 2019
Drones by CS.

Cory Strand.title: Les Miserables
Cory Strand.about:
A collaborative album by me and The Ebony Tower, based on Victor Hugo's novel and Raymond Bernard's film. Mitchell and I began working on this years ago (2017), and it has finally been completed. The source tracks were created by me utilizing the score to the Bernard film. Those tracks were then given the HNW treatment by Mitchell.
Super deluxe, very limited edition, self-released 34-disc set (containing both my ambient source tracks and the collaborative HNW tracks) in the works. Preorder available soon.
Cory Strand.credits:
released November 27, 2019
Music by Cory Strand and Mitchell Rotunno.

Cory Strand.title: Melancholia: A Miscellany
Cory Strand.about:
A small collection of practice space recordings, plus a live song and some home demos.
We've only played two live shows in our entire existence as Dreamless; we're mostly a studio project.
This was our live band circa 2012.
It was a blast.
In the immortal words of Gene Simmons, "I love it loud."
Cory Strand.credits:
released December 11, 2019
Songs 1, 2, 3:
Adam Patterson - guitars
Matthew Rolfe - drums
Cory Strand - guitars, vocals
Dustin Tessier - bass
Ben Zientara - guitars
Song 4, 5:
Cory Strand - all instruments/vocals
Songs 1, 2, 4, 5 recorded by Cory Strand.
Song 3 recorded by Jon Rosenthal.
All music written by Cory Strand.

Cory Strand.title: Ourselves: A Reinterpretation
Cory Strand.about:
Drone rework of an awesome but obscure metalcore compilation featuring live sets from seven different bands. I was very into this shit then, still love a lot of it to this day; I bought this CD in 1997 and it's a remained a favorite document of mine since. I especially liked how it interspersed samples from "Dead Poets Society" throughout, so the whole thing felt like a sort of focused statement on expectation/sorrow/disenchantment/suicide.
I've kept those samples intact in my reworks to give it that same cinematic sense of reach.
Cory Strand.credits:
released September 10, 2022
Music by CS.

Cory Strand.title: Potternalia
Cory Strand.about: This collection gathers all of the original noise and synthesizer work I composed for my two epic albums, "Harry Potter and the Order of the Phoenix: A Reinterpretation" and "Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince: A Reinterpretation." Much of my work in both drone and noise utilizes source materials; this collection highlights my own approach to noise and drone composition without manipulating pre-existing elements. "Into The Pensieve" and "Courage And Sacrifice" are new pieces composed specifically for this digital release.
Cory Strand.credits:
released February 14, 2015
All instruments and performance by Cory Strand.

Cory Strand.title: Pretension and Release, Vol. Five
Cory Strand.about:
(Ben) This show was one of the first we booked by just walking in to the place and making contact with a guy. The month before we played, I went to Bon Apetit to see a show and a band called Harkonen played. After the gig I walked up to one of the guys in the band and said "you guys are great! we should play a show together sometime!" "Dude, we're from Seattle," came the reply. I felt pretty dumb.
Anyway... we asked our friends The Knotwells to open the show. It was their first show ever! They went on to be much more popular than we could hope to be.
(Cory) My recollections of this are pretty vague. I do recall that my pedal rig for this show consisted of 17 pedals. Fucking ridiculous. I probably used about five of them, but I was trying to sell myself as some sort of effects wizard back then and really wanted visual effect. A weird set list, too. I'm not really sure what we were going for other than just leveling the audience. This show was in a very small space and was insanely loud. We used full stacks...
The pointless chatter caught on tape towards the end of the set are the inane mutterings of an associate of ours long since dispelled from our lives. His name was Taylor, and he was one of the most arrogant people I've ever met. I have no idea who he's talking to at this show. It was more than likely any breathing female within arm's length. I remember taking Taylor to a Mudhoney show with me once and being disgusted watching him make out with some random chick from First Ave during the encore. Part of me would like to make a documentary about him, because he's a weird, grossly fascinating presence. But that would mean getting in touch with him again. I'm not ready to go there.
I recall that Tool were playing a show this same night, and we somehow thought that was affecting out local draw. Jesus.
Cory Strand.credits: released May 22, 2012

trackinfo.title: Corazon (Live at Bon Apetit)
trackinfo.about:
(Ben) Another run-through of the perennial live fave. Vocals are particularly terrible on this take. I always hated the lyrics for this song, too. Lame come-ons and dirty phrases I learned in Spanish. Ask me what they mean sometime.
(Cory) As i said, we played this live quite a bit. "Corazon" had a solo that never really changed during any given run-through (it amazes me how bad I was about that-kinda like Kirk Hammett) but i did use a bunch of different effects every time. This featured the Zvex Ooh-Wah, which i bought after seeing David Scott Stone use one during a Melvins show. I pretty much modeled my guitar setup after his because it blew me away. This take also features the aborted ending.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Five, track released May 22, 2012

trackinfo.title: Waxing/Waning (Live at Bon Apetit)
trackinfo.about:
(Ben) I have always loved this song, and it's sad we never got a really good take of it recorded well. This one turns out okay, though.
(Cory) There's a mistake in the second section. One of us misses a chord and the whole thing seems to be playing backward after that. This was my brother's favorite KD track. It was really one of our more powerful live vehicles.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Five, released May 22, 2012

trackinfo.title: Paint the Moon with Blood (Live at Bon Apetit)
trackinfo.about: (Cory) My vocals were approaching something close to tuneful on this take. The delays get a little wonky after the guitar solo, but once Matt kicks it in everything seems to fall into place.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Five, released May 22, 2012

trackinfo.title: Intro to Psych (Live at Bon Apetit)
trackinfo.about:
(Ben) One of my favorite tracks to play, live or otherwise. Part of that enjoyment was because I got to be the STAR. Plus I wrote a good portion of the thing. As much as Cory laments being "the guy who plays the same solo every time" in any given song, it was something I aspired to with this song, and I was never able to produce the same results every time.
The microphone with which I was recording this show falls over mid-song, and our buddy Kyle, who we had charged with watching the level meters on our mixer, picked it up, shouting "it was on the GROUND!" to some other person. We're real pros.
(Cory) This is a slow version of this, and totally lacking in the spacey midsection that for me sort of defines the song. I'm wondering if at this time the track wasn't entirely worked out and were just testing it out live...I can't think of any other reason why it would have been excised.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Five, released May 22, 2012

trackinfo.title: Something in the Way (Live at Bon Apetit)
trackinfo.about:
(Ben) This is a super-heavy take of this track. Wow. That's all I have to say about that.
(Cory) I think we all loved playing this song. Much like "Youth of America," we really made it our own. I always preferred yelling as opposed to singing, and this song was perfect for that. Must have been another latent Slayer influence.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Five, released May 22, 2012

trackinfo.title: The Campbell's Soup Kids Have Finally Had Their Fill (Live at Bon Apetit)
trackinfo.about:
(Ben) THIS MY TRACK, YO. So fun to play.
(Cory) Wow. Insightful. Not sure that i can can really follow that up, but from a purely technical standpoint, we're playing this slow. I ripped part of my solo off in this song from Billy Corgan. Try to guess where. I was never a huge SP fan, but my brother once told me, in an accusing tone, that all of my music came back to the Pumpkins. This take also features the Ooh-Wah prominently, especially in the ending. The pedal crapped out on me a few months later. I don't even know if I have it anymore.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Five, released May 22, 2012

Cory Strand.title: Pretension and Release, Vol. Four
Cory Strand.about:
This was our second show. We were invited to play by our friends in the Amish Armada, and since it was at 7th. Street Entry (one of the best venues in Minneapolis) we jumped at the chance. I know I was really nervous and it shows in my behaviour at the show. There's a reason why I've since stopped chatting with an audience. Ben's much better suited for it and much funnier. We were really playing the songs slow at this point and they were aways from sounding their best, but this is still a milestone show for a few reasons. I had just bought my Fender Jaguar (still my main guitar) and was astonished at how awesome it sounded, although i really shot myself in the foot by using a Boss Hyperfuzz as my distortion on this one (yuck! what the fuck was I thinking?) Favorite quote from this show: Ben saying "It's all good, boys" before we ripped into "Youth of America." We later pressed this show up in a small edition-Matt created the artwork and handpainted the CDs. Sometimes I still see a copy turn up used in a Cheapo and it makes a little sad.
Fun fact: "Adrift in the Stars" was the first of our many domain names.
Cory Strand.credits: released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: The Epic/Fashion Bug Girls (Live at Seventh Street Entry)
trackinfo.about: We were really into creating medleys of our songs when we first started out, and this is one example. Obviously we were trying to make an entrance too, with Ben and I going it solo and having Matt come in as the song was gaining intensity. I really miss having a trem bar on my guitar-i used to wail on that fucker (i paid for it when it snapped off in my hand at a show a few years later.) I should just get a new one. This is probably the only live performance of "Fashion Bug Girls" featuring Ben's guitar solo. Worth it for that alone!
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Four, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Paint the Moon with Blood (Live at Seventh Street Entry)
trackinfo.about: My singing was getting a little better by this time. Everything else was sounding rough. I was pretty caught up in my own misery at this time, as evinced by my declaring the song was about someone who hates me.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Four, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Corazon (Live at Seventh Street Entry)
trackinfo.about: Pretty flattening version of "Corazon." This actually features a bit of the "ambient" ending we were trying to work in to the song and ultimately abandoned. Dig the minor league backing vocals I throw in.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Four, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Youth of America (Live at Seventh Street Entry)
trackinfo.about: "This one's for the kids."
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Four, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Orchid Garden (Live at Seventh Street Entry)
trackinfo.about: I know it seems silly, but there weren't a lot of bands doing what we were doing in Minneapolis at the time. Ben and I really felt like we were tapping into something special, and this demolishing run through "Orchid Garden" really captures the heart of what we were attempting back then. Listen to Matt go crazy at the end. I loved playing with him!
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Four, track released May 16, 2012

Cory Strand.title: Pretension and Release, Vol. One
Cory Strand.about: We'd only been together for about a year, but Ben and I got caught up in our own grandeur pretty quickly. Matt was always more grounded about everything. We'd settled on going sans bass after several botched attempts to get the right person and were steadily improving. In December of 2001, broke but hungry to get something out (and feeling Minneapolis needed to hear what we were doing), we decided to try and record our debut album ourselves. We holed up in our practice space for the night and let our friend Kevin Samuel commit it all to tape. We all liked the record but ultimately held out for a professional recording. This serves as a document of what "Threnodic" might have been had we not dialed in our egos a little bit. I remember this era of the band pretty fondly-all of us were really into what we were doing and it felt really aggressive and immediate. Ben and I were still playing with shitloads of pedals (my distortion on these recordings was me running a TurboRat alongside a Big Muff) and thinking we were the heirs apparent to Sonic Youth...there are moments here that I would never trade.
Cory Strand.credits:
released May 16, 2012
Music written and performed by Cory Strand, Ben Zientara, and Matt Rolfe. Recorded by Kevin Samuel, 12/14/2001.

trackinfo.title: Paint the Moon with Blood
trackinfo.about: It's kind of amazing to me how little this song changed. I never thought I would be the kind of person that played a guitar solo the same way every performance, but by the time we laid this version down the solo was set and I rarely deviated from it. Also amazing to me is how truly awful my vocals were before working with Adam on the record proper. I wrote this song immediately after hearing "Motr" by Trans Am. Lyrically it's no secret to anyone who knows me who this song was about, and I think when she first heard it, she accused me of immaturity. She was right, but I was fucked up and depressed then and didn't have any other way to channel it.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. One, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Fashion Bug Girls
trackinfo.about: Probably what many people would consider the quintessential Kafka Dreams track (my dad still asks me if we're going to play this one at every show we schedule.) I think this song really captured the ethos of what we were doing-the Mogwai style build-up wed to the Dinosaur Jr. type guitar inferno, with a bit of melancholy holding everything together. This version features a second guitar solo by Ben, which I always liked. He and Matt both felt all that soloing made the song run way too long (although on Ben's end I think he was being really self-critical.) I know they were right, but something about the excess of it really appeals to me, like I was appeasing my latent Slayer influence.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. One, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Corazon
trackinfo.about: I love this song. It really saddens me that it never made it onto the album proper, because it fucking slays-it was one of the funnest songs to play and it was everything we did distilled into three minutes. This was one of Ben's tracks, and I think it got nixed from the record because he was being too self-critical again-worried about his singing (don't know why, because he was and is better than me) or his songwriting (it's no secret this track owes a huge debt to Bardo Pond's "Tommy Gun Angel")-without realizing that he'd whipped out a total sludge rock assault. When we first started practicing and arranging "Corazon," it had an extended clean outro section full of echoes and delays that we could never really get to go anywhere, so we dropped it. I think the result is super visceral, and one of the band's strongest tracks.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. One, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: The Campbells' Soup Kids Have Finally Had Their Fill
trackinfo.about: A live favorite. This was one of the best instances of Ben and I writing something together-he brought in the hook and I built the song around it-but we would argue a lot over what was the more important part of the song. I was something of an asshole back then and wasn't very gracious about sharing credit. We've always had a soft spot for this particular version of the song, because there's the section in the middle where the drums drop out and it's just guitars roaring away, all sad and forlorn. I think our friend Ryan prefers this take, too. It was a mistake that we could never replicate, so we never tried. Matt came up with the title.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. One, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Intro to Psych
trackinfo.about: Another one of Ben's songs. Both he and Matt really liked this one. I wasn't so into it, for purely selfish reasons (I didn't get to do any soloing in it, and it was all clean guitar) but we played it live quite a bit. The middle section was always fun to do because it changed constantly, and was probably the place where Ben and I both began to utilize all of our effects in a meaningful way, as opposed to just making a bunch of noise. For whatever reason, Ben felt he could really cut loose on this song, and I think his playing on this track foreshadows his current style pretty well. Matt's drumming on this one was insane too, way over the top and all over everywhere. It was always fun to hear him flex his jazz muscles.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. One, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Waxing/Waning
trackinfo.about: Another instance of Ben and I writing together (I came up with the second section, Ben did the first, and we both contributed to each other's.) This was also a blast to play live but the timing was a serious fucker, and we could never get a version recorded that we felt was up to our standards. Either my feedbacking or Ben's delayed guitar would never come out right. Even the version we did with Adam for the album proper got cut, and it wasn't until Ben told me a few months ago that I even remembered we had tried to record it for "Threnodic." Matt's drumming here is killer. Total bombast. I think this one really sapped him live. One of my favorite KD tracks, easily.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. One, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Isobel
trackinfo.about: A crushed out ode to Bardo Pond's Isobel Sollenberger disguised as a suicide anthem. This song was also conceived as an illustrative response to the Meowth/Meowzy episode of "Pokemon" (look it up-it's a heartbreaker.) This was one of Matt's favorites because it was the only one of our songs that really displayed any true dynamic range, or allowed us to play quietly. This was played live every so often and usually became brutally loud because of all the echoes and delays. I wrote this song after hearing Mogwai's "Hugh Dallas." I was trying to write something very pretty but ultimately very loud and despondent. The lyrics were based on a poem I read in high school. I don't remember who wrote it or what it was called, but it hit me incredibly hard and informed a lot of my lyrical aestheticism.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. One, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Days of Plague
trackinfo.about: Purely me, and very indicative of my musical interests at the time. This was my take on Neurosis. It took a lot of convincing to get Matt to warm up to this one, but once he did we played it live a lot, and he really destroyed it every time. Again, I'm struck by how awful the vocals were. I was still trying to "sing" this when we recorded with Adam, and he asked that I try putting more "oomph" into it. I have no idea why I hadn't thought of that approach myself. Maybe because I cannot fucking sing for the life of me. We used to discuss the prospect of bringing in an actual vocalist, and I wouldn't give in to it because I couldn't envision someone else singing my songs, or letting someone else put words to my music. To a certain degree I still can't. Perhaps it's egotistical of me, but the music I write is first and foremost mine, and I have a hard time not controlling it entirely.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. One, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Orchid Garden
trackinfo.about: Our standard show closer. This was probably the first song Ben and I wrote together, because I distinctly remember jamming on it with Matt the first time we met him. Stupidly simple but evocatively effective, "Orchid Garden" kind of represents an apex for the Kafka Dreams. This was the closest we got to fusing Mogwai with Slayer with Merzbow, as well as our paean to Sonic Youth's "The Diamond Sea." Ben and I never got tired of bathing audiences in noise during this track. KD at their most destructive.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. One, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Something in the Way (Bonus)
trackinfo.about: Our cover of the classic Nirvana track. This version was recorded during the same time as the "12/14/01" material but not intended for the album. See the Volume Two liner notes for more specifics.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. One, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Youth of America (Bonus)
trackinfo.about: Our version of the seminal Wipers anthem. This version was recorded during the same time as the "12/14/01" material but not intended for the album. See the Volume Two liner notes for more specifics.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. One, track released May 16, 2012

Cory Strand.title: Pretension and Release, Vol. Three
Cory Strand.about: This is my favorite live show from the KD. Everything seemed to be on this night: we played tighter and more furiously than we ever had, and we were locked in to each other. We played a bunch of new stuff, too. It was really exciting and pretty much reinforced how much I needed making and playing music to be a part of my life.
Cory Strand.credits: released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Fanfare (Live at Big V's)
trackinfo.about: This was written specifically for this show, and we never played it again. Which is a serious bummer, because I really like it.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Three, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: The Growing Sadness (Live at Big V's)
trackinfo.about: I consider this to be the definitive take of "The Growing Sadness" in the absence of any official recording. Raw and visceral from the start, the track just shreds. I remember one night Ben and I were zoned out on mushrooms, driving into Wisconsin, listening to this song. When the section of my solo came on where it's all the tremelo noise/panning, Ben just turned to me and said, "Holy shit, Cory." Obviously we were susceptible to our own bravado and we were tripping, but it still strikes me as powerful. I never played the solo better.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Three, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Skates (Live at Big V's)
trackinfo.about: Searing live take of Ben's thrasher.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Three, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Indie Rock Girls and Their Stupid Boyfriends
trackinfo.about:
This was one of my very favorite KD songs, and it really saddens me we never got a chance to properly record it. Though the lyrics are accusatory, it's just me whining about the things I wanted and what I felt the universe was denying me for some reason. I was really lonely. The first verse references Pandora coffee house, a cool shop on Hennepin Avenue that no longer exists. I always wanted to meet a girl there. I never did.
I think this is some of Matt's best drumming. He SLAYS it on this song, and we were totally in synch, anticipating each other.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Three, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Rayz-----> (Live at Big V's)
trackinfo.about: Another great version of "Rayz----->" featuring some triplet riffing from yours truly towards the end. I could never figure out how to play the descending melody that I came up with in this version, although i kept trying every time we played this after. Must have been some magic in the air. I really love how this track just devolves here until it sputters out. Seems kind of apocalyptic.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Three, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Orchid Garden (Live at Big V's)
trackinfo.about: The majestic closing track. Audio destruction. We were inclined to try and crack the sky with this one.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Three, track released May 16, 2012

Cory Strand.title: Pretension and Release, Vol. Two
Cory Strand.about: This volume of the collection gathers all of the available b-sides and outtakes we could muster together. Most of the material was recorded during the "Threnodic" session, but there are a few other recordings included. As a band we tried to choose our covers pretty carefully: Matt and I both had backgrounds in punk and metal, and all three of us were Nirvana devotees, plus we all thought it pretty neat for a band so inclined towards "pretty" music to shred it up thrash style. At one point, Ben and I were tossing around the idea of a cover album of classic hardcore punk songs, but it never happened. I think we would have been pretty adept at it. There were a couple other covers we did that we regrettably don't have recordings of: for a while we were doing a massive cover of the Cure's "Plainsong" (which Matt hated playing as it sounded too much like our own material), Marvin Gaye's "Sexual Healing" (which Ben always rocked the vocal on), and at one show we did an improv-heavy riff on 5ive's "Bicycle Racer." All of those are lost to the ages. This stuff is what's left.
Cory Strand.credits: released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Corazon ("Threnodic" Outtake)
trackinfo.about: The studio version of Ben's sludge pop masterwork. I'm still mystified as to why this didn't make the record, especially since Ben did the sequencing for it. I can only guess he still wasn't sure about his singing or writing. I would gladly have taken off "Frustrated" ( a song I wrote but can't say I actually enjoy at this point) to have this track on "Threnodic" instead. As stated, this was a blast to play, and stands as one of my favorite KD tracks.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Two, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Youth of America "("Threnodic" Sessions)
trackinfo.about: Probably the cover that's closest to my heart. We'd been doing this Wipers song forever, after I heard the Melvins totally kill it at a few shows in 2001. It was everything I was wanting in a song: heavy as fuck, fast, long, psychedelic, and hugely anthemic. Using the Melvins version as a template, we amped it up a little and made it a regular part of our set. This sort of stuff was super fun to play. I got to really embrace my sloppy Greg Ginn meets EVH style rock and roll soloing (listen to the finger-tapping!) and even tried to reference Jello Biafra towards the end with the weird quaver in my voice. I think all of us really gravitated towards the aggression in this one, and I'm glad it's finally getting out there for you to hear. When we were mastering it, our engineer Dave Gardner total us we sounded like a Swedish hardcore band on this track. I take that as a pretty stellar compliment.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Two, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Waxing/Waning ("Threnodic" Outtake)
trackinfo.about: A studio take of this notoriously difficult track. Listening to this version now, it's pretty we clear why we left it off the album-there's a few mistakes throughout (which always seemed to happen as this song was very easy to lose yourself in) and I know the feedback in the beginning certainly wasn't where I wanted it to be. I still like that we actually have a studio recording, though. I had forgotten about it until just a few months ago, when Ben reminded me we had it. At least Matt was on point during this run through. He was always the best "musician" of the three of us.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Two, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: The Epic, Intro ("Threnodic" Outtake)
trackinfo.about: I have no idea why we recorded this. In the earliest stages of the band we were playing an enormous 25 minute song we referred to as "The Epic," and this was the intro to it. The actual song was ridiculously simple, just four chords built up forever and then ground into the dirt for about 15 minutes, with some of my nonsense lyrics about black clouds in the sky and feeling depressed. I'm trying to round up an old tape with it; hopefully Ben still has it around. I'm not sure we ever played it live, although we sometimes used this guitar intro as an opener (Ben and I would take the stage and play through it, then Matt would Saunter on and we'd kick into something full force.) I feel like Ben wanted to record this in the studio, but I'm not entirely sure...it strikes me as a sort of naive attempt at Mogwai style guitar interplay.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Two, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Something in the Way ("ATS, ATK" Session)
trackinfo.about: Shortly after the Kafka Dreams formed, we got involved with a small label from Madison who were putting together a Nirvana tribute album comprised of Midwestern bands. I had corresponded with the guy who was curating it and we were all locked in to contribute our version of "Something in the Way," stretched into a 12 minute slowcore epic. A few weeks later the label folded and I never heard from the guy again. All of us were really into playing this one live and were kind of sad we never were able to get it out. When we were doing the Dreamless album in 2006, we took a little break and banged out a couple covers as the Kafka Dreams, this being one of them. I think some of us prefer the "12/14/01" version; this one is a bit more tempered. Regardless, it was nice to finally have a studio take of a song that had been part of our live set for so long.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Two, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: D-7 ("ATS, ATK" Session)
trackinfo.about: The second KD track we recorded during the Dreamless session in 2006, another Wipers cover by way of Nirvana. Matt and I had both covered virtually every Nirvana song in our old bands; we both agreed that it got to a point where we were trying to pick the most obscure tracks from them to prove our dedication, or some such bullshit. This track, off of "Hormoaning," was always a favorite for both of us since it was so primal and aggressive. I think we do a pretty outstanding job on this, and it is certainly my favorite vocal performance from any of my recordings.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Two, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: The Growing Sadness (Live on Radio K 12/10/2006)
trackinfo.about:
When we originally released "Threnodic" we spent a lot of time trying to get the University of MN radio station, Radio K, to pick up the record. We were told the songs were all too long for broadcast (which I knew was bullshit because they would play "Didn't We Deserve a Look at You the Way You Really Are" by Shellac pretty regularly) and were promptly dismissed. A few years later a couple guys started a program called "Now Like Photographs" that focused on instrumental bands and invited us to play a session. By this time we hardly ever played anything off "Threnodic" but Matt thought this would be a good opportunity to maybe sell some of the (many) leftover copies we had, since we never played a release show for it. I thought the opposite and didn't want to play any of the old stuff; I thought we could just mention "Threnodic" and that would be good enough. I was wrong, because during the interview after the performance we were asked if any of the material we had played was on our record. I felt pretty stupid. At least I got to play a Skullflower track on the radio, though.
"The Growing Sadness" was slated to be the lead off track on the second record. It was a live favorite-I think we played it almost every show. This version isn't as aggressive as I would have liked and my clean guitar sounded a little weak, but those are egotistical concerns. When my dad heard this on the radio, he said it just sounded like a bunch of noise (in a good way.)
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Two, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Skates (Live on Radio K 12/10/2006)
trackinfo.about: This was another of Ben's songs, and a live favorite as well. I think we all dug this one because it was so short and fast and really stood in contrast to a lot of our other material. My favorite part was easily the ridiculous rock and roll riff that comes in at the end of the song and then just stops. I had originally wanted to extend that part quite a ways and put a huge guitar solo over it, but Ben and Matt nixed it. As usual, they were both right.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Two, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Rayz-----> (Live on Radio K 12/10/2006)
trackinfo.about: Another of Ben's tracks-by this point he was writing a lot more, with a voice totally different than mine. "Skates" and "Rayz----->" had originally been worked on as one long track that Ben and I were jamming on in a project that would eventually take shape as Yog-Sothoth. "Rayz----->" made it into a lot of live sets as well, primarily because it was one of the most open songs we had. I don't think it had any really set course or ending, and each time we performed it, it changed. This version highlights our growing use of sample pedals to augment our trio sound, allowing the guitars to go off a little more without worrying about rhythm.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Two, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Failure Song (Practice Space Recording, 2006)
trackinfo.about: This was one of the last things we were working on before we more or less collapsed into hiatus. I had come up with the main riff on bass at Ryan's house out in Seattle and really liked the simple direction of it. Maybe this was the precursor for Dreamless. I didn't think it then, but the one time we played this song at a show, Ben's wife commented that it sounded more like a Dreamless track than a KD one. I got pissed at that (more ego making me an asshole, again) but I now know Krista was correct. This song will more than likely be on the second Dreamless album, but here it is in a raw, infant stage, sans lyrics.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Two, track released May 16, 2012

trackinfo.title: Rayz-----> (Practice Space Recording)
trackinfo.about: A massive run through of "Rayz----->" recorded during the same time as "Failure Song." I think we were going to try and use this as a little demo to drum up a label. I think this is a pretty awesome version of the track, with monolithic guitars. I remember really liking this sound, but I've always favored more raw, blown-out recordings.
trackinfo.credits: from Pretension and Release, Vol. Two, track released May 16, 2012

Cory Strand.title: Salival (Extended): A Reinterpretation
Cory Strand.about:
It could be *argued* that this is my favorite Tool record. I've decided to tweak reality and augment the album a bit with some reworks of fairly hard to come by cover songs the band has been known to perform over the years.
Attribution for the curious:
"You Lied" originally recorded by Peach.
"No Quarter" originally recorded by Led Zeppelin.
Those two you probably know.
"Bboom" originally recorded by King Crimson.
"Some Days It's Dark" originally recorded by Death Lurks.
"Spasm" originally recorded by Peach.
"Commando" originally recorded by The Ramones.
"Demon Cleaner" originally recorded by Kyuss.
"Stranglehold" originally recorded by Ted Nugent.
"Holiday In Cambodia" originally recorded by the Dead Kennedys.
Cory Strand.credits:
released February 4, 2020
Music by CS.

Cory Strand.title: The Bling Ring: A Reinterpretation
Cory Strand.about: One of my absolute favorites in my discography! A reconstruction/deconstruction of the soundtrack for Sofia Coppola's film "The Bling Ring." Sofia's one of my faves in film, a brilliant director, and she knows how to put together an impeccable soundtrack. This release was one of the first collaborations between myself and my partner. It was released as a super-limited/lavish 6CD + bonus CD box set back in 2013, with a bunch of original art prints and extras. From the early days of AOW, when running the label didn't feel like perpetually drowning. This is where I first started experimenting with drums and HNW, which laid the groundwork for Fantome de Sang. Also worked with a lot of hip-hop material here, which was new for me. I love this one. Enjoy.
Cory Strand.credits:
released March 8, 2021
Music by CS. Artwork and visuals by GN.

Cory Strand.title: The Complete Theodore Robert Bundy: 2013 - 2014
Cory Strand.about:
This compilation collects all the HNW and drone/ambient work I created dealing with Ted Bundy. I've been fascinated by criminology since I was in my teens, it was my first major in college, and like so many others starting in the HNW world, I used that interest as subject matter. Bundy continues to be a disturbingly fascinating figure to me.
All of these recordings were issued under my old moniker Lethe; for this collection I've chosen to release them under my own name. All of this material has been previously released, through Altar Of Waste Records and Occult Supremacy Productions.
I've given each track a specific image as well; just choose each track proper to view.
Tracks 1-4: Originally released as "Theodore Robert Bundy" via Altar Of Waste Records in 2013.
This was the very first Altar Of Waste release (AOW 001)!
I had been dabbling with HNW since 2011, and had released a few things digitally as well as on a cassette compilation issued through Worthless Recordings, but these tracks comprised my first major physical release.
All of the walls here are crude, overly aggressive, caustic, dirty, sludged out, and not especially complex or nuanced in any way. It's very primitive stuff, an artist finding their footing. The last track, "Memories Of Necrophilia," points more towards the direction my work would take over the years.
The source material for all of these four pieces was guitar, played by me. I always took it as a point of pride that reviewers thought my walls sounded akin to Sunn 0))) and Earth, that the guitars were discernible in the walls. I'm a guitarist before anything else, so I'm glad that's always come through in my work.
Tracks 5-6: Originally released as "Confessions 1989, Volume One" via Occult Supremacy Productions in 2013.
Working with OSP was great as Dustin was always incredibly encouraging. I felt he really wanted me to do what I wanted to do and explore the sounds I wanted to pursue, regardless of whether they were walls or drones.
The source material for these pieces were Bundy's taped confessions to Robert Keppel and William Hagmeier, detailing the abduction and murder of Georgeann Hawkins.
Tracks 7-8: Originally released as "Confessions 1989, Volume Two" via Occult Supremacy Productions in 2014.
Deep drone material.
The source materials for these pieces were again Bundy's confession tapes to Keppel and Hagmeier, this time detailing the abduction, murder, and disposal of Donna Gail Manson.
Tracks 9-16: Originally released as "The Stranger" via Altar Of Waste Records in 2014.
This set consisted of extremely long-form recordings, both HNW and ambient/drone material. It was meant to be punishing and exhaustive, and very hollowed out.
The tracks were so long that the recordings were released on DVD-R data discs (not uncommon for me at the time; I was very into creating severely long and demanding pieces.)
I don't recall the source material used for this album. Lost to the ages. Some of it sounds like guitars ("Bundy Revisited I and II") but I can't specifically remember.
I don't offer these tracks here as some sort of revelation or anything; they're just early work i did, when I was starting down this path. I still like these albums quite a bit; I think a lot of us in the HNW world have a tendency to cringe over some of our earlier wall work, but I'm not embarrassed by these recordings at all. Quite the opposite. This is where it all started for me: these walls, this set, this subject matter. They're valuable to me as archival material; it's fun to see where I began as an artist and designer. It's also humbling to remember that I can always learn more, can always refine my process and my craft, can always push further. Just keep going.
-Cory Strand, January 2020
Cory Strand.credits:
released January 7, 2020
Everything by Cory Strand.

Cory Strand.title: The Dark Knight: A Reinterpretation

This is one of my very favorite films ever, one of my favorite scores ever, and a personal high point in my discography.

Only two physical copies exist.
This is one of my recordings that I am most proud of.

Cory Strand.title: Two Riders Were Approaching
Cory Strand.about:
An exhaustive study in source material. Each piece is sourced from a different live recording of Neil Young performing Bob Dylan's timeless "All Along The Watchtower," from 1992 - 2008. Backing bands include Crazy Horse, the E Street Band, and Booker T. and the MG's. Different groupings performing the same material, directed under the same visionary.
My transformative process for each track was the same. All sonic differences are the results of varying recording/bootleg quality and the individual nature of each performance, based on Neil's synergy with his co-performers and the ineffable quality of "the moment."
I could have edited the tracks in a more ambiguous manner, cutting off obvious starts and ends and creating more opaque swathes of sound, but there's no point in obscuring what the song is. I could have provided attribution regarding each performance. I don't really want to. It seems unnecessary to me in regards to the higher aim of the collection. This is a study in specificity. You can hear each piece build and fold in on itself, a reflection of each performance as an isolated and unrepeatable instance in time/era. Does it really matter when that time/era is?
This sort of recycling has been endlessly fascinating to me; there's reference to it in my chosen artwork for this release, in that those familiar with my work (ha!) will note that I've used the cover image (from Neil's "Journey Through The Past" score) prior. I hope there's some sort of beauty and wonder in all of this pastiche and reinterpretation; please get lost in it. The wind begins to howl.
Cory Strand.credits:
released September 18, 2020
Music by CS.
